source.title: Encoding specificity and retrieval processes in episodic memory

confidence_rationale: Foundational, robustly-replicated principle that is the formal core of MECH-338: a retrieval cue is effective to the extent it overlaps the operations performed at encoding. Maps almost verbatim onto 'a cue is matched against context tags written at population time; matching candidates become rollout-eligible'. High confidence; held at 0.82 (not higher) because the principle is general-memory, the REE object is a context-tagged candidate RULE rather than an episodic item, and the rollout-eligibility gate is a REE-side construct.
failure_signatures: A strong pre-experimental associate that was NOT the encoding context fails to cue an item that a weak but encoding-matched cue retrieves -- cue strength per se is not what governs access; encoding-context match is. A retrieval gate that scored candidate availability by raw cue salience rather than by match to the tags written at population time would mispredict this (the recognition-failure-of-recallable-words result).

If the operations performed at encoding determine what is stored and therefore which cues are effective, a population mechanism that writes a candidate WITHOUT binding it to its scaffolding context provides nothing for a later cue to match -- context-free population breaks cued retrieval.

mapping.source_claim_statement: Specific encoding operations performed on what is perceived determine what is stored, and what is stored determines which retrieval cues are effective in providing access to it. A cue (even a normally strong associate) is effective only to the extent it matches the trace as encoded; cues present and encoded with the target are effective even when weak.
mapping.ree_translation: MECH-338 is the 'select' face of the ARC-063 field: a cue is matched against the context tags a CandidateRule was given at population time (MECH-337), and matching candidates are promoted toward rollout-eligibility. Encoding specificity is the formal principle behind exactly this: a rule scaffolded in context C is retrievable later iff the present cue overlaps the encoded context C -- which is why context-bound population (MECH-337) is the precondition that makes cue-driven retrieval pay off, and why the discriminative cut can be a function of episode/agent history (sharpening ARC-063 falsifiable-prediction a).
mapping.mapping_caveat: Tulving & Thomson concern episodic memory for word lists in adult humans; the REE object is a context-tagged candidate RULE feeding a rollout-eligibility gate, not an episodic recollection. The principle transfers cleanly at the level of mechanism (cue<->encoded-context match governs access) but MECH-338's specific gate (Tolerance-Principle availability still decides final eligibility; MECH-338 only supplies the cue-addressed candidate set) is a REE construct the paper does not speak to.
mapping.source_context: Adult human verbal episodic-memory experiments (cued recall vs. recognition; the recognition-failure-of-recallable-words paradigm).

confidence_components.notes: Source quality very high: foundational, repeatedly replicated, theory-defining. Mapping fidelity high -- the principle is essentially MECH-338's mechanism stated in memory-science terms. Transfer risk low-moderate: episodic-item memory -> context-tagged candidate-rule retrieval is an analogical but well-motivated mapping; the rollout-eligibility gate itself is REE-specific.
